description: Okay, I've got an important problem for you to work on. We need to have really good on-device text-to-speech. So set that up. I think we did it before in the ATG monorepo, and I think also potentially somewhere else. But yeah, basically use an open-source text-to-speech model. This one's way better than the built-in iOS text-to-speech. So use a much better one of that. We may in the future end up pushing that off device, but for now, I think just on here is okay.

results.identified_issue:
Prior art was NOT in the ATG monorepo (that's Flutter, and only uses flutter_tts / Apple system voices). The real implementation is /Users/jakeschantz/buyahabit @ 7f820c526: Kokoro-82M -> sherpa-onnx offline-TTS C API -> ONNX Runtime (CPU) -> Float32 PCM -> hand-rolled WAV. I ported that stack rather than picking a newer runtime (CoreML/MLX/an SPM package) precisely BECAUSE this flow forbids building: an unproven runtime I cannot link-test is strictly worse than one already known to work in a sibling repo with identical XcodeGen tooling.

Key design call: instead of swapping one speech transport for another, SpeechRenderer synthesizes to a WAV file and the EXISTING AVPlayer path plays it. This was not just aesthetic — the old TTS path had to fake duration (ArgumentDeckBuilder's 140wpm estimate), fake progress (spoken-character count), and could not scrub (a backward seek re-spoke from a word offset). Rendering to a file makes all three real and collapses the isTTS branch to a fallback. The voice-3b3f9453 invariants (read-only bar, engine-truth state, backward-only seek, completion gating) are preserved because rendered speech now flows through the very code that already satisfied them.

Latency is the risk I could not measure. Kokoro on CPU is seconds for a long argument. Mitigation is prefetch: DiscoverView.renderSpeechAhead synthesizes the next 3 text answers while the current card plays, so only the first card of a session can outrun it (it shows a spinner). If that first-card wait feels bad on device, the cheap fix is to fall back to AVSpeechSynthesizer immediately on a cache miss rather than waiting — one branch in ClipPreviewPlayer.play.

Speaker is hardcoded to 5 (Adam, US male) in SpeechRenderer; the bundle has 11 voices (0-10), so a voice picker is a one-line change away.

VERIFICATION (important for whoever inherits this): I did NOT run a build — the flow forbids it. What I DID verify: (1) the whole app type-checks against the real iOS SDK with the bridging header via 'swiftc -typecheck' (this caught a real bug: NS_ERROR_ENUM imports as KokoroTTSError.Code, not KokoroTTSErrorCode); (2) KokoroTTS.mm compiles clean against the real sherpa-onnx header with clang -fsyntax-only, so __has_include resolves and the C API usage matches this build of sherpa-onnx; (3) all three symbols the bridge calls are present and exported in the arm64 device slice via nm. What remains UNVERIFIED: the actual static link, and any runtime/device behavior. If vigil's build fails it is almost certainly linkage, not Swift.
results.summary: Swapped Apple's synthesizer for on-device Kokoro-82M, ported from the buyahabit stack — and rendered speech now plays as real audio, so the scrubber finally tells the truth.
results.detailed_description:
You were close but the prior art wasn't in ATG — that one's Flutter and only wraps Apple's system voices. The real thing was in buyahabit: Kokoro-82M running through a vendored sherpa-onnx + ONNX Runtime. I ported that stack here, since it's already proven on the same XcodeGen tooling.

The interesting part isn't the model swap, it's what it let me delete. The old TTS path had to fake everything: duration was a 140-words-per-minute guess, progress was a count of characters spoken, and you couldn't scrub at all — a backward seek just re-spoke from the nearest word. So instead of swapping one speech engine for another, Kokoro renders an answer to a WAV file on disk and the audio player you already had plays it. Spoken text is now just an audio file. The scrubber spans real audio, the duration is real, and 'you must hear this all the way through before it unlocks' now keys off actual playback instead of an estimate.

Synthesis takes a few seconds on a long argument, so the feed renders the next three answers ahead while you're listening to the current one. In practice only the very first card of a session can outrun it, and that one shows a spinner.

Two things to expect on your first launch. The weights are 351MB and download in the background, so until they land you'll still hear the old Apple voice — there's a 'Downloading the better voice' progress bar in the feed while that happens. It's verified by checksum before it's used, so a dropped connection leaves nothing rather than a broken model. Second, this added about 178MB of vendored binaries to the repo (the .a files for sherpa-onnx and ONNX Runtime, plus the espeak G2P data). That's the same trade you made in buyahabit, but it's the one thing here that's annoying to undo later, so say the word if you'd rather fetch them in a build script instead.

I want to be straight about what I did and didn't check. I'm not allowed to build in this flow, so I never linked the app. What I did do: type-checked the entire app against the real iOS SDK through the bridging header (that caught a genuine bug — the error enum imports into Swift under a different name than I'd written), compiled the Objective-C++ bridge against the actual sherpa-onnx header, and confirmed the symbols it calls are exported in the arm64 device slice. So the Swift and the bridge are sound. What I couldn't exercise is the final link and anything at runtime. If the build breaks, it'll be linkage, and I'll fix it next turn.

The voice is Kokoro's 'Adam' (US male). There are 11 in the bundle — easy to switch, or to put behind a picker, if you want a different read.
